mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that simplifies the management of Kubernetes clusters through declarative APIs. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to provision, upgrade, and operate multiple clusters consistently across various infrastructures. Key trade-offs include the complexity of initial setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This is the official repository for Cluster API Helm charts.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Cluster API to function.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This YAML file contains all necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API components using Helm, which will manage the lifecycle of your clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

```

> Ensure you are installing in the kube-system namespace.
commands: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Cluster API custom resource to define your desired cluster configuration.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProviderCluster' with your actual infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> You should see the Cluster API components running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API components
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this may lead to data loss for any managed clusters.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API components
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your cluster configurations. This step performs the upgrade and verifies the installation.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o yaml > cluster-backup.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup your current cluster configurations.
> Upgrade to the latest version.
> Verify that the upgraded components are running.
commands: helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD has not been installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the Cluster API CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api-components.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually means that the Kubernetes API server is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Check your kubeconfig file and ensure your cluster is running: kubectl cluster-info

troubleshooting.title: Pods in kube-system are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if there are misconfigurations in the Cluster API components.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the failing pods: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system

troubleshooting.title: Cluster creation fails with 'timeout'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the infrastructure provider is not responding or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Verify your infrastructure provider settings and ensure that resources are available.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved the stability of existing components.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old API versions for infrastructure providers.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your custom resources to the new API versions.

resolution.summary: You should see the Cluster API components running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system' showing all pods in 'Running' state.
codeSnippets: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and the necessary tools installed.
